title: End behavior of rational functions
description: Given a rational function, determine how it behaves as x approaches +‚ąě or -‚ąě.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{7x^2-2x}{x+5}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{7x^2}-2x}{\purpleD{x}+5}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{7x^2}}{\purpleD{x}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{7x^2}{x}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{7x^2}{x} =\dfrac{7\cancel{x}\cdot x}{\cancel{x}}=7x$ 

What does $7x$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $7x$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

 $7x\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $7x$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

$7x\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-8x^3-3x+4}{2x^2-9}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-8x^3}-3x+4}{\purpleD{2x^2}-9}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-8x^3}}{\purpleD{2x^2}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-8x^3}{2x^2}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-8x^3}{2x^2} =\dfrac{-4\cdot\cancel 2\cdot \cancel{x^2}\cdot x}{\cancel 2\cdot \cancel{x^2}}=-4x$ 

What does $-4x$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $-4x$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

 $-4x\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $-4x$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

$-4x\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{9x^4-5x^2-x}{3x-7}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{9x^4}-5x^2-x}{\purpleD{3x}-7}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{9x^4}}{\purpleD{3x}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{9x^4}{3x}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{9x^4}{3x} =\dfrac{3\cdot\cancel 3\cdot\cancel{x}\cdot x^3}{\cancel 3\cdot \cancel{x}}=3x^3$ 

What does $3x^3$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $3x^3$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

 $3x^3\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $3x^3$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

$3x^3\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-6x^5+8x^4+3x}{6x^2-4x-5}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-6x^5}+8x^4+3x}{\purpleD{6x^2}-4x-5}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-6x^5}}{\purpleD{6x^2}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-6x^5}{6x^2}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-6x^5}{6x^2} =\dfrac{-1 \cdot\cancel 6\cdot \cancel{x^2}\cdot x^3}{\cancel 6\cdot \cancel{x^2}}=-x^3$ 

What does $-x^3$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $-x^3$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

 $-x^3\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $-x^3$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

$-x^3\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{6x^3-x^2+7}{2x+5}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{6x^3}-x^2+7}{\purpleD{2x}+5}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{6x^3}}{\purpleD{2x}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{6x^3}{2x}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{6x^3}{2x} =\dfrac{3\cdot\cancel 2\cdot\cancel{x}\cdot x^2}{\cancel 2\cdot \cancel{x}}=3x^2$ 

What does $3x^2$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $3x^2$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

 $3x^2\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $3x^2$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

$3x^2\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-8x^4+3x^2-7x}{4x^2-4}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-8x^4}+3x^2-7x}{\purpleD{4x^2}-4}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-8x^4}}{\purpleD{4x^2}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-8x^4}{4x^2}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-8x^4}{4x^2} =\dfrac{-2\cdot\cancel 4\cdot\cancel{x^2}\cdot x^2}{\cancel 4\cdot \cancel{x^2}}=-2x^2$ 

What does $-2x^2$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $-2x^2$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

 $-2x^2\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $-2x^2$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

$-2x^2\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{9x^5-2x^2-3x}{x-6}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{9x^5}-2x^2-3x}{\purpleD{x}-6}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{9x^5}}{\purpleD{x}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{9x^5}{x}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{9x^5}{x} =\dfrac{9\cdot\cancel{x}\cdot x^4}{\cancel{x}}=9x^4$ 

What does $9x^4$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $9x^4$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

 $9x^4\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $9x^4$ gets larger in the positive direction. We write this as:

$9x^4\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-5x^6+8x^5-1}{x^2-9x}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-5x^6}+8x^5-1}{\purpleD{x^2}-9x}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-5x^6}}{\purpleD{x^2}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-5x^6}{x^2}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-5x^6}{x^2} =\dfrac{-5\cdot\cancel{x^2}\cdot x^4}{ \cancel{x^2}}=-5x^4$ 

What does $-5x^4$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $-5x^4$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

 $-5x^4\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $-5x^4$ gets larger in the negative direction. We write this as:

$-5x^4\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to -\infty$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{2x^2+5x}{-6x^4+3x^3-7}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{2x^2}+5x}{\purpleD{-6x^4}+3x^3-7}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{2x^2}}{\purpleD{-6x^4}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{2x^2}{-6x^4}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{2x^2}{-6x^4} =\dfrac{\cancel 2\cdot\cancel{x^2}}{-3\cdot\cancel 2\cdot \cancel{x^2}\cdot x^2}=-\dfrac{1}{3x^2}$ 

What does $-\dfrac{1}{3x^2}$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $-\dfrac{1}{3x^2}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

 $-\dfrac{1}{3x^2}\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $-\dfrac{1}{3x^2}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$-\dfrac{1}{3x^2}\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-x^4+6x^2+4x}{-3x^5+x^4-8}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-x^4}+6x^2+4x}{\purpleD{-3x^5}+x^4-8}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-x^4}}{\purpleD{-3x^5}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-x^4}{-3x^5}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-x^4}{-3x^5} =\dfrac{\cancel{-1}\cdot\cancel{x^4}}{\cancel{-1}\cdot 3\cdot \cancel{x^4}\cdot x}=\dfrac{1}{3x}$ 

What does $\dfrac{1}{3x}$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $\dfrac{1}{3x}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$\dfrac{1}{3x}\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $\dfrac{1}{3x}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$\dfrac{1}{3x}\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-4x^3+7x+9}{8x^6-9x^4-2x}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-4x^3}+7x+9}{\purpleD{8x^6}-9x^4-2x}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-4x^3}}{\purpleD{8x^6}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-4x^3}{8x^6}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-4x^3}{8x^6} =\dfrac{-1\cdot\cancel{4}\cdot\cancel{x^3}}{2\cdot\cancel{4}\cdot \cancel{x^3}\cdot x^3}=-\dfrac{1}{2x^3}$ 

What does $-\dfrac{1}{2x^3}$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $-\dfrac{1}{2x^3}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$-\dfrac{1}{2x^3}\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $-\dfrac{1}{2x^3}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$-\dfrac{1}{2x^3}\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{x-5}{5x^3-2x^2+3}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE x-5}{\purpleD{5x^3}-2x^2+3}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{x}}{\purpleD{5x^3}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{x}{5x^3}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x}{5x^3} =\dfrac{\cancel{x}}{5\cdot\cancel{x}\cdot x^2}=\dfrac{1}{5x^2}$ 

What does $\dfrac{1}{5x^2}$ approach as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$?

As $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, the value of $\dfrac{1}{5x^2}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$\dfrac{1}{5x^2}\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, the value of $\dfrac{1}{5x^2}$ gets closer and closer to zero. We write this as:

$\dfrac{1}{5x^2}\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

$f$ should behave the same.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-2x^2+3x-7}{-6x^2+9}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-2x^2}+3x-7}{\purpleD{-6x^2}+9}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-2x^2}}{\purpleD{-6x^2}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-2x^2}{-6x^2}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-2x^2}{-6x^2} =\dfrac{\cancel{-2}\cdot\cancel{x^2}}{\cancel{-2}\cdot 3\cdot\cancel{x^2}}=\dfrac{1}{3}$

This means that as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$, our function $f$ behaves like the constant function $y=\dfrac13$.

Specifically, as $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $\dfrac13$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to \dfrac13$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $\dfrac13$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to \dfrac13$ as $x\to \infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to \dfrac13$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to \dfrac13$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{-8x^3+5x^2-1}{2x^3-9x}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{-8x^3}+5x^2-1}{\purpleD{2x^3}-9x}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{-8x^3}}{\purpleD{2x^3}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{-8x^3}{2x^3}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{-8x^3}{2x^3} =\dfrac{-4\cdot\cancel{2}\cdot\cancel{x^3}}{\cancel{2}\cdot\cancel{x^3}}=-4$

This means that as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$, our function $f$ behaves like the constant function $y=-4$.

Specifically, as $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $-4$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to -4$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $-4$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to -4$ as $x\to \infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to -4$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to -4$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{x^4-4x^3+6x}{-5x^4+8x^2-3}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{x^4}-4x^3+6x}{\purpleD{-5x^4}+8x^2-3}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{x^4}}{\purpleD{-5x^4}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{x^4}{-5x^4}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{x^4}{-5x^4} =\dfrac{\cancel{x^4}}{-5\cdot\cancel{x^4}}=-\dfrac{1}{5}$

This means that as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$, our function $f$ behaves like the constant function $y=-\dfrac15$.

Specifically, as $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $-\dfrac15$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to -\dfrac15$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $-\dfrac15$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to -\dfrac15$ as $x\to \infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to -\dfrac15$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to -\dfrac15$ as $x\to \infty$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rational function $f$.

$f(x)=\dfrac{4x^5-6x^3+7x^2}{2x^5-x^4-5}$

**Determine $f$'s end behavior.**

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 1]] as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to$ [[‚ėÉ dropdown 2]] as $x\to \infty$.
hints:
The **end behavior** of a function $f$ describes the trend of the function at the "ends" of the $x$-axis, i.e., as $x$ approaches $+\infty$ and as $x$ approaches $-\infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 2]]

The end behavior of any polynomial is similar to the end behavior of the polynomial that only contains the [[‚ėÉ definition 1]].

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

For rational functions, the end behavior will be similar to the leading term in the numerator divided by leading term in the denominator.

In our case:

$\dfrac{\goldE{4x^5}-6x^3+7x^2}{\purpleD{2x^5}-x^4-5}\approx\dfrac{\goldE{4x^5}}{\purpleD{2x^5}}$

We can now reduce $\dfrac{4x^5}{2x^5}$ to lowest terms:

$\dfrac{4x^5}{2x^5} =\dfrac{2\cdot\cancel{2}\cdot\cancel{x^5}}{\cancel{2}\cdot\cancel{x^5}}=2$

This means that as $x$ approaches $\infty$ and $-\infty$, our function $f$ behaves like the constant function $y=2$.

Specifically, as $x$ gets larger in the negative direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $2$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to 2$ as $x\to -\infty$.

As $x$ gets larger in the positive direction, $f$ gets closer and closer to $2$. We write this as:

$f(x)\to 2$ as $x\to \infty$.

[[‚ėÉ explanation 1]]

This is $f$'s end behavior:

$f(x)\to 2$ as $x\to -\infty$.

$f(x)\to 2$ as $x\to \infty$.
